1: theme O God, we have heard with our ears, our fathers have told us, what deeds you performed in their days, in the days of old. The covenant history rehearsed here reaches its climax in Christ. What the fathers told of finds its fulfillment in these last days when God has spoken by his Son (Hebrews 1:1-2) — the final and definitive act in the drama of redemption.
2: theme You with your own hand drove out the nations, but them you planted; you afflicted the peoples, but them you set free. The divine warrior's historical acts point to Christ's ultimate victory: he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him (Colossians 2:15). The pattern of driving out and planting reaches its eschatological fulfillment in the new creation.
3: theme For not by their own sword did they win the land, nor did their own arm save them, but your right hand and your arm, and the light of your face, for you delighted in them. The principle of divine grace over human achievement is the theological foundation of the gospel: by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God (Ephesians 2:8-9). Salvation belongs to the LORD.
4: shadow You are my King, O God; ordain salvation for Jacob! Christ is the King who ordains salvation — the royal commission to save his people from their sins (Matthew 1:21). The kingship of Christ is the ground of salvation; he reigns so that he might redeem (Revelation 17:14 — Lord of lords who overcomes).
5: shadow Through you we push down our foes; through your name we tread down those who rise up against us. The name above every name (Philippians 2:9-10) — Christ's name — is the power through which death and sin are trampled. The disciples cast out demons in his name (Luke 10:17); Paul preaches in his name; the Name is the power of the new covenant community.
6: theme For not in my bow do I trust, nor can my sword save me. Renouncing military power as the means of salvation — fulfilled in Christ who refused the sword: Put your sword back into its place. For all who take the sword will perish by the sword (Matthew 26:52). Salvation comes not by the sword of armies but by the weakness of the cross (1 Corinthians 1:18).
7: theme But you have saved us from our foes and have put to shame those who hate us. God saves through Christ — the only Savior (Acts 4:12). Those who hated him are put to shame by the resurrection; those who crucified him must acknowledge truly this was the Son of God (Matthew 27:54).
8: theme In God we have boasted continually, and we will give thanks to your name forever. The boasting Paul grounds in Christ: let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord (1 Corinthians 1:31). The continual thanksgiving finds its fullest voice in the church gathered around Christ — giving thanks always and for everything to God the Father in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ (Ephesians 5:20).
9: shadow But you have rejected and disgraced us and have not gone out with our armies. The apparent rejection of God's people — applied by Paul to the present age of Israel's partial hardening (Romans 11:15) — participates in the mystery of the cross where God appeared to abandon his own Son (Matthew 27:46). The rejection is real but temporary; restoration follows.
10: shadow You have made us turn back from the foe, and those who hate us have gotten spoil. Israel's defeat in the lament points to Christ's apparent defeat at the cross — the enemies seemed to get the spoil. But the resurrection reverses it: he led a host of captives (Ephesians 4:8), taking back what had been seized.
11: shadow You have made us like sheep for slaughter and have scattered us among the nations. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 8:36 within the passage on Christ's sufferings. The scattered sheep appear in Jesus's Gethsemane prediction: I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep of the flock will be scattered (Matthew 26:31, citing Zechariah 13:7). The imagery connects Passion and apostolic suffering.
12: shadow You have sold your people for a trifle, demanding no high price for them. The pricing of the righteous one: Jesus is sold for thirty pieces of silver — the price of a slave (Matthew 26:15; Zechariah 11:12-13). God gives his Son for those who are worth far less than the price paid; the trifle for which the people were sold is answered by the infinite price of Christ's blood (1 Peter 1:18-19).
13: shadow You have made us the taunt of our neighbors, the derision and scorn of those around us. The mockery Christ endures — from Herod's soldiers (Luke 23:11), from the crowd at the cross (Matthew 27:39-44) — is the supreme instance of communal derision. The taunt Are you not the Christ? Save yourself! is the voice of the enemy at its worst.
14: shadow You have made us a byword among the nations, a laughingstock among the peoples. The crucifixion as public spectacle — Roman executions were designed to humiliate. Christ became a byword and laughingstock on the cross: a crucified Messiah was a stumbling block to Jews and folly to Gentiles (1 Corinthians 1:23). The shame of the cross is the wisdom of God.
15: shadow All day long my disgrace is before me, and shame has covered my face. Christ endured the cross, despising its shame (Hebrews 12:2). The disgrace before him all day long is the nakedness and humiliation of crucifixion — the most shameful death in the ancient world. He bore it so that our shame might be covered by his righteousness.
16: shadow At the sound of the taunting and reviling, at the sight of the enemy and the avenger. The Passion soundscape: taunts from the crowd, reviling from the soldiers, the high priest's mockery. Christ endured all — when he was reviled, he did not revile in return (1 Peter 2:23). The avenger (death) appears to triumph at Golgotha; resurrection overturns the appearance.
17: shadow All this has come upon us, though we have not forgotten you, and we have not been false to your covenant. The suffering of the innocent is supremely true of Christ. He is the perfectly covenant-faithful one who suffers precisely because of his faithfulness to the Father's plan (Philippians 2:8 — obedient to the point of death).
18: shadow Our heart has not turned back, nor have our steps departed from your way. Christ's unbending faithfulness: he set his face to go to Jerusalem (Luke 9:51) even knowing what awaited him. His heart never turned back — the perfect obedience that becomes righteousness for all who believe (Romans 5:19).
19: shadow Yet you have broken us in the place of jackals and covered us with the shadow of death. The shadow of death at Calvary — darkness covered the land from noon to three (Matthew 27:45). The place of jackals is the desolation of Golgotha. Christ is broken in that place of desolation, covered by the shadow that he came to abolish (2 Timothy 1:10).
20: shadow If we had forgotten the name of our God or spread out our hands to a foreign god, The conditional of innocence: the psalmist asserts they have not abandoned God. Christ never spread his hands to a foreign god — he spread his hands on the cross in absolute fidelity to the Father. His suffering is the purest expression of covenant faithfulness possible.
21: shadow would not God discover this? For he knows the secrets of the heart. God who knows the secrets of the heart vindicates Christ because he finds there absolute purity: Which of you convicts me of sin? (John 8:46). God declared Jesus righteous by resurrection — the ultimate vindication of a sinless heart.
22: direct Yet for your sake we are being killed all the day long; we are regarded as sheep to be slaughtered. Romans 8:36 quotes this verse explicitly: As it is written, For your sake we are being killed all the day long; we are regarded as sheep to be slaughtered. Paul frames it with the climactic declaration that nothing can separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus — the sheep for slaughter are held in the risen Christ's unbreakable grip.
23: shadow Awake! Why are you sleeping, O Lord? Rouse yourself! Do not reject us forever! The urgent awake — God apparently asleep while his people suffer — is lament at the extremity of affliction. The resurrection is God's awakening to act decisively: he who raised Jesus from the dead (Romans 4:24) vindicates the righteous sufferer and all who are in him.
24: shadow Why do you hide your face? Why do you forget our affliction and oppression? The hidden face of God — divine abandonment — is Christ's experience on the cross (Psalms 22:1; Matthew 27:46). The hiddenness is real but not final; the resurrection is the face of God turned again in favor toward the afflicted one and all who are in him.
25: shadow For our soul is bowed down to the dust; our belly clings to the ground. Humiliation in the dust — the language of death (Genesis 3:19). Christ descends to the dust of death (Psalms 22:15) and is laid in the earth (John 19:41-42). He who bowed to the dust is raised from it to sit at the right hand of the Majesty on high (Hebrews 1:3).
26: shadow Rise up; come to our help! Redeem us for the sake of your steadfast love! The final petition — rise up... redeem us — is answered in the resurrection of Christ. God rises to redeem by raising his Son. The sake of your steadfast love is the motive of the Incarnation and atonement (John 3:16). The rising and the redemption are inseparable.
